channel.title: Health on MinWeb
channel.description: Recent content in Health on MinWeb

item.title: Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome: Why Chronic Pain Persists
item.description:
For many, a mastectomy is strictly about survival. We focus on removing the cancer to guarantee a future, and we celebrate those life-saving results. Rightly so. But there is a silent chapter that begins after the cancer is gone and the hospital discharge papers are signed. For too many women, the hardest part of the journey isn’t the diagnosis—it’s the debilitating, chronic pain that follows.

If you’ve been told that persistent pain after surgery is just “part of the healing process,” know this: you aren’t losing your mind. An investigative report published on April 4, 2026, by USA TODAY and KFF Health News finally put a name to this condition that’s been hiding in plain sight: Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome (PMPS). Honestly, it’s about time someone started talking about this openly.

item.title: The Hidden Chronic Pain After Mastectomy Surgery
item.description:
For thousands of women, a mastectomy is a life-saving decision. It’s a courageous act to stop cancer and reclaim a future. But for a staggering number of these patients, the surgery isn’t the end of the battle; it’s the beginning of a silent, agonizing struggle that most surgeons never prepare them for.

Imagine waking up from a life-altering surgery only to find your own body feels like the enemy. You expect some discomfort, sure, but then the “stabbing” begins. Weeks pass, and simple, everyday actions—like lifting a fork, putting on a shirt, or even feeling the soft brush of a pet—send jolts of electricity radiating through your chest and back. This isn’t just “post-surgical soreness.” It is Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome (PMPS). Honestly, it’s infuriating that so many women are left to figure this out on their own. For far too many, this becomes a permanent, devastating reality.

item.title: Why Beans Are the Most Underrated Superfood
item.description:
You know the playground rhyme: “Beans, beans, the musical fruit, the more you eat, the more you toot.” It’s a catchy trope, but it’s turned one of the healthiest foods on the planet into a cheap punchline. Honestly, we’re doing ourselves a huge disservice by avoiding them.

There’s a massive gap between the “gassy” reputation of beans and their actual impact on our bodies. Only about 8% of U.S. adults eat legumes on any given day, but the data tells a completely different story. Look at the “Blue Zones”—those pockets of the world like Sardinia and Nicoya where people live the longest, healthiest lives. Beans are a foundational staple for these people.

item.title: Gym Story Saturday: Why Your Gym Anxiety Is Normal
item.description: If you’ve ever felt like the gym is less of a sanctuary and more of a social experiment gone wrong, you aren’t alone. We’ve all been there: staring at a machine we don’t know how to use, watching someone occupy an entire row of dumbbells for their “private” workout, or wondering if that guy in the leather jacket is actually training or filming an avant-garde music video. Honestly, some of these people act like they’re the only ones who pay a membership fee.

item.title: Why We’re Still Hooked on Full-Sugar Soda
item.description:
If you’ve ever wandered down the soda aisle, you’ve likely felt a twinge of bewilderment. We live in an era where the health risks of sugar are common knowledge. We know that sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) trigger rapid insulin spikes, contribute to Type 2 diabetes, damage the liver, and hijack the brain’s dopamine system to create genuine dependency.

And yet, the global market for carbonated soft drinks is projected to reach $473 billion by 2025. Even more shocking? As of 2024, full-sugar sodas still hold over half of that market.

item.title: Nutrition Myths in 2026: Why You Can Stop Fearing Eggs
item.description:
It’s 2026. If you’ve spent any time on social media or in wellness forums lately, you know the feeling: nutrition advice has become a minefield. One week, the algorithm claims intermittent fasting is the key to longevity; the next, you’re reading that your morning eggs are sabotaging your heart. It’s exhausting.

The core of our frustration is that we’ve spent years obsessing over individual nutrients or “villain” foods in isolation. We hunt for a singular culprit—gluten, carbs, sugar, or cholesterol—while ignoring the broader context of our lifestyle, gut health, and social environment. (Personally, I’m over the constant fear-mongering.)

item.title: The Biggest Nutrition Myths We Finally Debunked in 2026
item.description:
If you’ve opened social media in the last year, you’ve likely seen someone telling you to stop eating fruit because of “sugar spikes,” avoid gluten because it’s “inflammatory,” or jump into an intermittent fasting window to “unlock autophagy.”

It’s exhausting, isn’t it?

According to a January 2026 survey by EatRightPro, 80% of Americans find it genuinely difficult to tell fact from fiction when it comes to nutrition. With nearly half the population getting their health advice from influencers and one-third turning to AI-generated meal plans, misinformation has become the main course of our national conversation.

item.title: The Biggest Nutrition Myths We Still Believe in 2026
item.description:
If you’ve spent any time on social media or health forums lately, you know the feeling: nutrition advice has become a frantic, contradictory mess. One week, the internet claims eggs will ruin your heart. The next, an AI-generated meal plan tells you to double your protein intake because you’re “wasting potential.” It’s exhausting.

As of January 2026, the 2025–2030 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (DGA) have finally provided some much-needed clarity. Yet, despite the updated science, many of us are still clinging to outdated “food rules” that were debunked years ago.

item.title: Why “Everything in Moderation” Fails Some People
item.description:
If you cannot stop at one cookie, stop blaming your willpower. For about 20 percent of people, the standard advice to eat everything in moderation is not just unhelpful—it is a setup for failure.

When you eat ultra-processed food, your brain gets a two-stage dopamine hit: once when you taste it, and again when it hits your gut. If your brain is wired as an Abstainer, that single bite acts as a chemical primer. It flips a switch that moves your decision-making from your logical prefrontal cortex to your primal, reward-seeking lower brain. Psychologists call this the What-the-Hell Effect. Once that seal is broken, your brain stops asking for a snack and starts demanding a flood. Honestly, trying to moderate these foods is like trying to convince a fire to stop burning once you have already poured gas on it.

item.title: The Case for Body Silence: Why You Should Stop Commenting
item.description:
Stop talking about people’s bodies. Even if you think you are being kind, you are crossing a line that has serious consequences for the other person’s health and mental well-being. Practicing Body Silence is the most respectful thing you can do for the people around you.

_IMG_PLACEHOLDER

Minding your own business is a public health intervention. When you comment on a body, you have no idea what that person is enduring. You might be triggering an eating disorder, insulting a grieving parent, or stressing someone out so much that their body enters a defensive, high-stress state.

item.title: Why You Should Stop Commenting on People’s Bodies
item.description:
The most important thing you can do for the people in your life is remarkably simple: mind your own business.

Stop commenting on bodies. This applies to the ones you think you are complimenting and the ones you find confusing. Even if you think you are being supportive, your words carry weight you cannot see and history you do not know.

_IMG_PLACEHOLDER

The unintended cost of your words

Saying “You look great, have you lost weight?” is not a harmless way to make someone feel good. A 2024 University of Bristol study found that weight teasing at age 13 predicts higher internalized weight stigma and poorer mental health twenty years later.
